title: Somatic Therapy for Survivors of Childhood Emotional Neglect by Yoga Culture
excerpt: Discover how somatic therapy helps survivors of childhood emotional neglect heal by reconnecting body and mind for lasting recovery.
body:
Understanding Childhood Emotional Neglect (CEN)

Childhood Emotional Neglect (CEN) is a hidden trauma that often goes unrecognized. Unlike overt abuse, CEN involves a caregiver’s failure to respond adequately to a child's emotional needs. This absence leaves a child feeling unseen, unheard, and emotionally invisible, resulting in profound difficulties with self-regulation, self-worth, and relational trust later in life. Psychologist Jonice Webb, a leading researcher on CEN, describes it as a "failure to provide enough emotional support, nurturance, and validation" during critical developmental periods.1

Survivors of CEN often report feeling numb, disconnected from their emotions, or unable to identify or express feelings. These symptoms are not just psychological — they manifest physically in the body and nervous system, which is why somatic therapy can be particularly effective.

Why Traditional Talk Therapy Isn't Enough

Many survivors of CEN have tried traditional talk therapy and still feel stuck. This is because CEN affects implicit memories stored in the body rather than explicit memories accessible through conscious recall. As Bessel van der Kolk, a pioneer in trauma research, explains, trauma is “stored in the body” and cannot be fully processed through language alone.2 When emotional neglect is internalized, the body learns to suppress or disconnect from feelings as a survival strategy. Talk therapy may help with intellectual understanding but often misses this somatic imprint of trauma.

Somatic Therapy: Reconnecting Body and Mind

Somatic therapy focuses on the body’s sensations, movements, and autonomic nervous system responses as pathways to healing trauma. Peter Levine, founder of Somatic Experiencing, emphasizes that trauma recovery requires acknowledging and releasing the physiological energy trapped in the body.3 For survivors of CEN, somatic approaches can help identify where emotional numbness or shutdown is held physically, and gently guide the nervous system back to regulation.

Yoga culture, with its emphasis on breath, posture, and mindful movement, has incorporated many somatic principles. But somatic therapy is not about yoga poses alone—it’s an embodied awareness practice that cultivates safety, presence, and emotional attunement within the body.

The Neurobiology of Emotional Neglect and Somatic Healing

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory provides a neurobiological framework for understanding the impact of emotional neglect on the nervous system.4 In the absence of emotional attunement during childhood, the autonomic nervous system can become dysregulated, often defaulting to defensive states like shutdown (dorsal vagal) or hypervigilance (sympathetic activation). Survivors may struggle to access the ventral vagal state, which supports social engagement, safety, and emotional connection.

Somatic therapy helps survivors access and strengthen the ventral vagal pathways through gentle, body-based interventions. This can include breath regulation, movement, and mindful awareness practices that co-regulate and soothe the nervous system—a process Deb Dana calls “nervous system tuning.”5

Practical Somatic Techniques for Survivors of CEN

Incorporating somatic techniques builds the capacity to feel emotions safely and regulate bodily states. Some effective practices include:

  • Body Scan Meditation: Developing interoceptive awareness by slowly observing physical sensations without judgment.
  • Breath Regulation: Using diaphragmatic breathing to activate the parasympathetic nervous system and reduce anxiety.
  • Grounding Exercises: Engaging the body in physical sensations like feeling feet on the floor to anchor presence.
  • Movement and Stretching: Gentle yoga or somatic movement to release tension and increase body awareness.
  • Tracking Emotions in the Body: Noticing where emotions manifest physically rather than trying to intellectualize or avoid them.

Pat Ogden’s Sensorimotor Psychotherapy integrates these approaches with trauma processing, emphasizing safety and titrated exposure to bodily sensations and emotions.6

Why Yoga Culture Supports Somatic Healing for CEN

Yoga culture, when approached mindfully, offers a supportive container for somatic healing. It promotes embodiment, self-compassion, and attunement to inner experience. However, survivors of CEN should be cautious: some yoga practices can inadvertently trigger dysregulation if they push too quickly into emotional vulnerability without adequate support.

Integrating trauma-informed principles into yoga means prioritizing choice, safety, and gradual exploration of sensations. This aligns with the research of Francine Shapiro, who found that eye movement desensitization and reprocessing (EMDR) is effective because it allows the brain to reprocess traumatic memories safely.7 Similarly, trauma-informed yoga and somatic therapy create safe pathways for the brain and body to integrate neglected emotional experiences.

Challenges and Considerations in Somatic Therapy for CEN

Healing from CEN through somatic therapy is not linear or quick. Survivors may experience:

  • Initial overwhelm when beginning to notice emotions previously suppressed.
  • Resistance or skepticism about body-based approaches if accustomed to intellectualizing feelings.
  • The need for consistent, ongoing practice to rebuild nervous system regulation.

Working with a trauma-informed somatic therapist or yoga instructor trained in trauma-sensitive approaches can help manage these challenges. Dan Siegel’s work on integration stresses the importance of linking mind, brain, and body in a coherent way for lasting healing.8

“Somatic therapy allows survivors of emotional neglect to reclaim their bodies as safe and trustworthy allies in their healing journey.” — The Oracle Lover

Building a Somatic Healing Practice

For self-directed healing, survivors can start by:

  • Setting aside daily time for body-based mindfulness or gentle movement.
  • Journaling sensations and emotions noticed during somatic practices.
  • Exploring trauma-informed yoga classes or workshops.
  • Seeking professional support when feeling overwhelmed.

Remember, as Gabor Maté emphasizes, compassion towards oneself is essential to healing early relational wounds.9 Somatic therapy is not about forcing feelings but creating space to feel safely and integrate those feelings into the whole self.

Conclusion

Childhood Emotional Neglect leaves invisibly deep wounds that manifest in body and mind. Somatic therapy, supported by trauma-informed yoga culture, offers a science-backed pathway to reconnect with the body, regulate the nervous system, and reclaim emotional life. This holistic approach addresses what talk therapy alone often cannot: the implicit, somatic memory of neglect. With patience, safety, and practice, survivors can transform numbness into presence and disconnection into embodied resilience.

References

  • Webb, J. (2013). Running on Empty: Overcome Your Childhood Emotional Neglect.
  • van der Kolk, B. (2014). The Body Keeps the Score. Penguin Books.
  • Levine, P. (1997). Waking the Tiger: Healing Trauma. North Atlantic Books.
  • Porges, S. (2011). The Polyvagal Theory. Norton & Company.
  • Dana, D. (2018). The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy. Norton & Company.
  • Ogden, P., Minton, K., & Pain, C. (2006). Trauma and the Body. Norton & Company.
  • Shapiro, F. (2017). Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) Therapy. Guilford Press.
  • Siegel, D. (2012). The Developing Mind. Guilford Press.
  • Maté, G. (2018). In the Realm of Hungry Ghosts. North Atlantic Books.

faqs.q: What is childhood emotional neglect and how does it affect the body?
faqs.a: Childhood emotional neglect occurs when a caregiver fails to meet a child's emotional needs, leading to emotional disconnection. This neglect imprints on the nervous system, causing bodily shutdowns, numbness, and difficulties regulating emotions.

faqs.q: How does somatic therapy differ from traditional talk therapy for CEN?
faqs.a: Somatic therapy focuses on body sensations and nervous system regulation, addressing trauma stored physically. In contrast, talk therapy primarily works with conscious thoughts and memories, which may not reach the implicit body-based trauma of CEN.

faqs.q: Can yoga alone heal childhood emotional neglect?
faqs.a: Yoga can support healing by fostering embodiment and nervous system regulation, but it should be trauma-informed and combined with somatic awareness practices. Without proper guidance, some yoga practices might inadvertently trigger trauma responses.

faqs.q: What are some practical somatic techniques survivors can use at home?
faqs.a: Techniques include body scan meditations, diaphragmatic breathing, grounding exercises, gentle movement, and tracking emotions in the body. These help rebuild safety and emotional awareness gradually.

affiliateProducts.title: The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk
affiliateProducts.description: A foundational text explaining how trauma is stored in the body and how somatic therapies can facilitate healing.
